table_of_contents.title: Testing whether language-model reasoning is real
Agent terminology and pre-LLM instruction following
Language models as policies and web-agent benchmarks
Synthetic demonstrations from exploration and relabeling
Multimodal agents, failure modes, and recap

units.name: Reasoning taxonomy and prompt-based reasoning
units.description: Defines the kinds of reasoning used in the lecture and introduces prompting methods that elicit stepwise reasoning from language models.
units.summary: The lecture frames reasoning as using facts and logic to reach answers, then distinguishes deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning with simple examples, while noting that the lecture will mainly focus on informal deductive reasoning [ts=80s][ts=177s]. It then asks whether large language models can reason and introduces chain-of-thought prompting as a way to have the model emit intermediate reasoning before the answer, either through in-context exemplars or the zero-shot cue "let's think step by step" [ts=248s][ts=283s]. Two extensions are covered: self-consistency, which samples multiple rationales and selects the most common answer because agreement across diverse rationales is treated as a correctness signal [ts=305s][ts=355s], and least-to-most prompting, which decomposes a harder problem into sub-questions, answers each sub-question, and conditions the final answer on those results; this can generalize from short demonstrations to problems with many more steps, although strong prompt engineering can sometimes match it [ts=449s][ts=562s].

key_points.text: Deductive reasoning derives a firm conclusion from premises and logic, while inductive and abductive reasoning infer likely conclusions or explanations from observations.
For the rest of the lecture, reasoning mainly means informal deductive reasoning with multiple steps.
Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to produce a reasoning step before the final answer, either from demonstrations or with the cue "let's think step by step."
Self-consistency samples multiple rationales and chooses the most common answer instead of greedily decoding one rationale.
Least-to-most prompting first decomposes a question into sub-problems, answers them, and then composes the final answer from those sub-answers.

units.name: Reasoning distillation and iterative self-training
units.description: Explains how reasoning traces can supervise smaller models and how a model can improve itself by generating and filtering its own rationales.
units.summary: The lecture next asks how to transfer reasoning behavior into smaller models and presents Orca, which fine-tunes a 13B LLaMA model on GPT-4 explanations generated from FLAN-v2 instructions, questions, and answers [ts=603s][ts=724s]. The supervision is not just the answer but the full step-by-step explanation, illustrated with a median-calculation example, and the central idea is that a smaller model can imitate the explanatory behavior of a stronger model [ts=705s][ts=732s]. To evaluate reasoning beyond grade-school math, the lecture introduces BigBench-hard, a 23-subtask benchmark that includes Boolean evaluation, date understanding, and geometric-shape questions from SVG paths; Orca is described as outperforming Vicuna and even ChatGPT on this benchmark because it is specialized on explanation-rich reasoning data [ts=758s][ts=963s]. The segment then turns to reinforced self-training (REST), where a model samples multiple rationales, keeps those that yield correct answers, fine-tunes on them, and repeats the cycle; the reported results show gains on GSM8K and MATH, and in some settings iterative self-training can outperform training on human-provided rationales [ts=1005s][ts=1207s].

key_points.text: Orca fine-tunes a 13B LLaMA model on GPT-4-generated explanations rather than answers alone.
Orca data is built by taking FLAN-v2 instructions and prompting GPT-4 or ChatGPT to produce informative step-by-step explanations.
BigBench-hard contains 23 multi-step reasoning subtasks, including Boolean evaluation, date reasoning, and geometric shape identification from SVG.
On BigBench-hard, Orca is presented as outperforming Vicuna and even ChatGPT because it is specialized on explanation data.
REST alternates between generating multiple rationales, filtering by answer correctness, and fine-tuning on the retained rationales.
Multiple iterations of self-training can improve performance and can surpass supervised fine-tuning on human rationales in some settings.

units.name: Evaluating faithfulness and systematicity in reasoning
units.description: Presents skepticism about apparent reasoning by testing whether rationales causally matter and whether performance survives out-of-distribution changes.
units.summary: After showing ways to induce reasoning-like behavior, the lecture asks whether the resulting rationales are faithful, meaning whether the model's answer actually depends on the rationale it produced [ts=1252s][ts=1276s]. Two diagnostic experiments are described: early exit, where the rationale is cut off after one or more sentences before forcing an answer, and rationale corruption, where mistakes are inserted partway through the rationale; when accuracy barely changes, the rationale is interpreted as a post-hoc explanation rather than a causal computation trace [ts=1306s][ts=1477s]. The lecture then proposes counterfactual or out-of-distribution evaluation by changing the setting while preserving the abstract skill, such as moving from base-10 arithmetic to base-9 arithmetic or changing world facts in logic problems, and reports sizable performance drops even on simple logic tasks [ts=1517s][ts=1642s]. A final example on analogical reasoning shows that changing the alphabet or the transformation rule sharply reduces model performance while humans degrade very little, leading to the cautious conclusion that current systems show some reasoning and some memorization but not robust systematic generalization [ts=1662s][ts=1782s].

key_points.text: A rationale is faithful only if the model's final answer actually depends on that rationale.
Early-exit testing forces the model to answer after only part of its rationale; unchanged accuracy suggests the omitted steps were not doing real work.
Corrupting an intermediate reasoning step and observing little answer change is another sign that the rationale may be post-hoc.
Base-9 arithmetic and altered-logic worlds are used as out-of-distribution tests for whether the model learned the underlying rule rather than memorized training patterns.
Analogical reasoning experiments show large drops for models under task or alphabet changes, while humans show little decrease.

units.name: Agent formulation and historical approaches
units.description: Introduces language-conditioned agents and contrasts modern agent work with pre-LLM approaches based on semantic parsing, planning, and reinforcement learning.
units.summary: The lecture then switches from reasoning to language-model agents and defines the basic setup: an agent interacts with an environment by receiving observations, taking actions, and conditioning on a language instruction g that specifies the task [ts=1819s][ts=1882s]. Example environments include browsers where the goal might be booking a flight, with observations represented as pixels or HTML DOM and actions such as typing, clicking, or moving the mouse; the speaker also lists digital assistants, natural-language programming, UI automation, media control, and plugin/tool use as applications [ts=1905s][ts=2025s]. Before modern LLM agents, the lecture says there were three main paradigms: mapping utterances to logical forms that could be executed against a database or knowledge graph, mapping instructions to executable plans that a separate execution model could run, and directly learning policies with reinforcement learning from language instructions and rewards [ts=2048s][ts=2324s]. This historical framing matters because the later LLM-based methods can be understood as replacing specialized parsers or RL policies with more general autoregressive models [ts=2323s][ts=2327s].

key_points.text: A language-model agent receives observations from an environment and a language instruction g, then issues actions conditioned on both.
In browser environments, observations can be raw pixels or HTML DOM, and actions include typing, clicking, and mouse movement.
One pre-LLM approach was semantic parsing from natural language into executable logical forms.
A second pre-LLM approach inferred higher-level executable plans from instruction-action pairs and then executed the plans with a dedicated execution model.
A third pre-LLM approach used reinforcement learning to learn policies that maximize reward conditioned on instructions and observations.

units.name: Language models as policies and agent benchmarks
units.description: Recasts action selection as generative modeling and surveys the main browser-style benchmarks used to test agentic behavior.
units.summary: To explain the modern 2024 view of agents, the lecture models a trajectory of states and actions conditioned on a goal, then factorizes that object into environment transition dynamics and an agent policy over the next action [ts=2339s][ts=2392s]. This motivates treating decision-making as a generative or causal language-modeling problem in which a transformer consumes the action history, current state, and task description and predicts the next action autoregressively [ts=2400s][ts=2444s]. The speaker then describes a simple prompting-loop agent that is given a text description of the action space, the instruction, and the action-observation history so far, and is repeatedly asked to output the next action; conceptually, this is chain-of-thought prompting run in a control loop [ts=2500s][ts=2553s]. The segment closes with benchmark environments: MiniWoB for short-horizon sandbox browser tasks, WebArena for more realistic sandboxed websites with tools and multi-tab browsing, and WebLINX for logged interactions on real websites with the added possibility of communicating back to the user [ts=2586s][ts=2773s].

key_points.text: A trajectory conditioned on a goal can be factorized into environment transition dynamics and an agent policy over actions.
Decision-making can be treated as autoregressive trajectory modeling, where the transformer predicts the next action from state, history, and task context.
A simple modern agent prompt includes the action space, the instruction, and the observation-action history, then asks for the next action in a loop.
MiniWoB is a short-horizon sandbox benchmark for simple browser tasks such as retweeting or forwarding email.
WebArena adds more realistic websites, external tools, and multi-tab browsing, while WebLINX uses real websites and allows the agent to communicate with the user.

units.name: Training agents with synthetic demonstrations
units.description: Shows how agent data can be bootstrapped by exploration, language-based relabeling, and iterative improvement instead of relying only on human demonstrations.
units.summary: The lecture first notes that standard practice for web agents is few-shot in-context learning with human demonstrations collected for each environment or site, but this is unscalable because environments and interactions are too numerous [ts=2811s][ts=2855s]. It then adapts an earlier reasoning idea to agents: start with an unconditioned model that randomly explores an environment to generate trajectories, use a second language model to describe what instruction each trajectory appears to satisfy, and treat those inferred instruction-trajectory pairs as supervision [ts=2879s][ts=2959s]. The process is iterative: once a tentative instruction is assigned, the model is conditioned on that goal to generate a better trajectory, a filter checks whether the trajectory matches the instruction, and even failed trajectories are relabeled to salvage whatever subtask they actually completed instead of being discarded [ts=2987s][ts=3123s]. The overall outcome is a synthetic-demonstration pipeline that can either fine-tune the policy or replace human in-context examples, with a reported 13-point improvement on MiniWoB and gains on another multi-step tool-use environment [ts=3132s][ts=3204s].

key_points.text: Human few-shot demonstrations are the default training signal for many agent settings, but collecting them for each new environment is not scalable.
An unconditioned agent can randomly explore a website to produce raw trajectories of clicks, typing, and scrolling.
A second language model relabels each trajectory with a natural-language description of the task it appears to accomplish.
If a generated trajectory fails the original task, it can be relabeled with the best matching subtask rather than thrown away.
Synthetic demonstrations can replace human in-context examples and improve MiniWoB performance by about 13 points.

units.name: Multimodal agents and current limitations
units.description: Extends agents from DOM/text inputs to image inputs and ends with a candid assessment of current benchmark gaps and common failure modes.
units.summary: The final segment motivates multimodal agents by arguing that feeding full HTML and JavaScript into context can be intractable, and that screenshots or pixels may sometimes be a better state representation for acting in interfaces [ts=3214s][ts=3249s]. It then sketches two vision-language models that have been adapted for agent use: LLaVA, which uses GPT-4-generated question-response supervision to jointly tune an image encoder and a text decoder, and Pix2Struct, which pretrains on website screenshots by masking regions and predicting the HTML of the masked elements [ts=3263s][ts=3441s]. The lecture emphasizes that current agent performance still depends heavily on bespoke prompting and few-shot examples, and that even on MiniWoB, long-horizon tasks degrade sharply as the number of required actions grows [ts=3461s][ts=3520s]. On harder settings such as WebArena and WebLINX, the gap to humans remains large, and models make trivial unrecoverable mistakes such as typing the email into the password field or repeating a search term multiple times; the recap then ties these limits back to the broader theme that both reasoning and agents remain open research problems [ts=3538s][ts=3812s].

key_points.text: For real interfaces, pixel or screenshot input can be preferable to feeding massive DOM and JavaScript contexts into the model.
LLaVA builds a vision-language model by using GPT-4-generated instruction-response pairs to jointly fine-tune an image encoder and a text decoder.
Pix2Struct pretrains on website screenshots by masking visual regions and training the decoder to output the HTML corresponding to the masked content.
Even with extensive prompting and few-shot examples, performance on MiniWoB drops as tasks require longer action sequences.
A representative agent failure is typing the email address into the password field and failing to recover.

section_flags.rationale: This opening unit defines the lecture's notion of reasoning and introduces the three prompting techniques that recur throughout later sections [ts=80s][ts=305s][ts=431s].
The segment gives concrete training recipes for transferring or bootstrapping reasoning behavior, including Orca and REST, and reports benchmark evidence for both [ts=639s][ts=1005s][ts=1207s].
This section is conceptually central because it tests whether reasoning traces are faithful and whether performance reflects generalization or memorization [ts=1252s][ts=1517s][ts=1782s].
The unit establishes the agent setting and contrasts it with older approaches, which is important background for the modern methods that follow [ts=1867s][ts=2048s][ts=2324s].
It presents the main modern abstraction of agents as autoregressive policies and names the principal benchmarks used to evaluate them [ts=2375s][ts=2517s][ts=2586s].
This is the lecture's most concrete agent-training pipeline and mirrors the earlier reasoning-trace supervision story through exploration, relabeling, and filtering [ts=2879s][ts=3088s][ts=3132s].
The final unit extends agents to visual inputs and closes with the main empirical takeaway that current models remain far below human reliability on real tasks [ts=3217s][ts=3461s][ts=3607s].

kp_catalog.name: Reasoning taxonomy and informal deductive reasoning
kp_catalog.description: The lecture distinguishes deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning and then narrows its focus to informal deductive multi-step reasoning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reasoning taxonomy and informal deductive reasoning clarifies the lecture distinguishes deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning and then narrows its focus to informal deductive multi-step reasoning, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
kp_catalog.description: A prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting. Prompting with worked intermediate reasoning steps so the model generates a scratchpad before the final answer.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chain-of-thought prompting formalizes how a prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between capstone and gateway and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Self-consistency decoding
kp_catalog.description: Sample multiple rationales and answers, then choose the most frequent answer as a more reliable prediction.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because self-consistency decoding clarifies sample multiple rationales and answers, then choose the most frequent answer as a more reliable prediction, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Least-to-most prompting
kp_catalog.description: A decomposition strategy where the model first breaks a problem into sub-problems, solves them, and then composes the final answer.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because least-to-most prompting clarifies a decomposition strategy where the model first breaks a problem into sub-problems, solves them, and then composes the final answer, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Distilling reasoning traces into smaller models
kp_catalog.description: Use a strong teacher model to generate step-by-step explanations and fine-tune a smaller model on those explanations.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because distilling reasoning traces into smaller models clarifies use a strong teacher model to generate step-by-step explanations and fine-tune a smaller model on those explanations, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Iterative self-training on rationales
kp_catalog.description: A model improves by generating many rationales, filtering for correct ones, fine-tuning on them, and repeating the cycle.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because iterative self-training on rationales clarifies a model improves by generating many rationales, filtering for correct ones, fine-tuning on them, and repeating the cycle, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Faithfulness and counterfactual evaluation of reasoning
kp_catalog.description: Reasoning quality should be tested by whether rationales causally affect answers and whether performance survives distribution shifts.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because faithfulness and counterfactual evaluation of reasoning clarifies reasoning quality should be tested by whether rationales causally affect answers and whether performance survives distribution shifts, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Language-conditioned agents
kp_catalog.description: An agent receives observations from an environment and a language instruction, then selects actions to complete the instructed task.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because language-conditioned agents formalizes how an agent receives observations from an environment and a language instruction, then selects actions to complete the instructed task, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Pre-LLM instruction-following paradigms
kp_catalog.description: Historical approaches include semantic parsing into logical forms, executable plans, and reinforcement learning policies.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because pre-LLM instruction-following paradigms clarifies historical approaches include semantic parsing into logical forms, executable plans, and reinforcement learning policies, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive trajectory modeling and prompting in a loop
kp_catalog.description: Model action selection as next-token prediction over state-action histories and repeatedly query the model for the next action.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive trajectory modeling and prompting in a loop formalizes how model action selection as next-token prediction over state-action histories and repeatedly query the model for the next action, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Agent benchmarks and evaluation environments
kp_catalog.description: MiniWoB, WebArena, and WebLINX provide progressively more realistic settings for measuring task success in browser-like environments.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because agent benchmarks and evaluation environments clarifies miniWoB, WebArena, and WebLINX provide progressively more realistic settings for measuring task success in browser-like environments, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Synthetic demonstrations through exploration and relabeling
kp_catalog.description: Generate agent training data by exploring environments, labeling trajectories with inferred instructions, and iteratively refining both.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because synthetic demonstrations through exploration and relabeling clarifies generate agent training data by exploring environments, labeling trajectories with inferred instructions, and iteratively refining both, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal UI agents
kp_catalog.description: Use vision-language models such as LLaVA and Pix2Struct so agents can act from screenshots or pixels rather than only text or DOM.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal UI agents clarifies use vision-language models such as LLaVA and Pix2Struct so agents can act from screenshots or pixels rather than only text or DOM, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Current limitations and human-model gap
kp_catalog.description: Despite progress, current reasoning and agent systems remain brittle, prompt-sensitive, and far below human reliability on longer tasks.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because current limitations and human-model gap synthesizes despite progress, current reasoning and agent systems remain brittle, prompt-sensitive, and far below human reliability on longer tasks into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.
